note: SOP knowledge game question bank. Every question is grounded in a corpus document; doc is the corpus document id shown as the source after answering. Hand-written July 2026 from the consolidated corpus; review after substantive SOP revisions.

categories.title: Intake & Stray Processing
categories.tagline: Holds, impounds, and getting pets back home

questions.q: A stray dog comes in with no license tag, no microchip, and no tattoo. How long is the Stray Wait hold?
choices: Four business days, not counting holidays, weekends, or the day of impound
Ten calendar days from the day of impound
72 hours including weekends
Seven calendar days from the day of impound
questions.why: Stray Wait animals are held four business days, and holidays, weekends, and the day of impound do not count toward the hold.

questions.q: A cat arrives with a microchip that traces to an owner. After notification is mailed, how long is the Owner Known hold?
choices: Four business days
Ten calendar days from the time notification is sent by first class mail
Five business days from the time the owner answers the phone
There is no hold once an owner is identified
questions.why: Owner Known animals are held ten calendar days from when notification is sent by first class mail, with a Chameleon status of Known Wait.

questions.q: What Chameleon status do you enter for a stray that has viable identification at the time of impound?
choices: SW for Stray Wait
OS for Owner Surrender
KW for Known Wait
RTO for Return to Owner
questions.why: An animal with a license tag, microchip, tattoo, or known owner information is statused KW, Known Wait.

questions.q: Every avenue to reach a Known Wait animal's owner has failed. What has to happen before you change the status to Stray Wait?
choices: Nothing, change it as soon as the mailed notice goes out
Supervisory approval
A 30 day waiting period
Written signoff from the shelter veterinarian
questions.why: The status changes from Known Wait to Stray Wait only after all contact avenues are exhausted and a supervisor approves.

questions.q: An owner arrives to reclaim their impounded dog. What do you ask for first to verify their identity?
choices: A utility bill with their address
A photo of the dog on their phone
A government issued identification
Their Chameleon Person ID number
questions.why: State law requires positive identification, so you request a government issued ID and match it against the owner information in Chameleon.

questions.q: A healthy dog is being reclaimed by its owner. What must happen before it can leave, under the rule in effect since January 1, 2022?
choices: The dog must be spayed or neutered
The dog must be microchipped, unless a veterinarian certifies in writing it is medically unfit for the procedure
The owner must complete an obedience class voucher
The dog must complete a ten day observation period
questions.why: No healthy dog or cat may be reclaimed without being microchipped unless veterinary staff certifies in writing that the animal is medically unfit for microchipping.

questions.q: A healthy four month old dog is impounded this morning. Which vaccines does it receive at impound?
choices: Rabies only
DHPP and an intranasal Bordetella
FVRCP and rabies
None until the stray hold expires
questions.why: All dogs older than six weeks receive DHPP and intranasal Bordetella at the time of impound or delivery to the shelter.

questions.q: Which vaccine do all cats older than six weeks receive at impound?
choices: FVRCP
DHPP
Bordetella
FeLV
questions.why: Cats older than six weeks receive an FVRCP vaccination at the time of impound, with case by case exceptions for safety.

questions.q: When does an adopted dog or cat receive its rabies vaccination?
choices: At the first visit to the adopter's own veterinarian
Only if it is going to a home with other pets
Before release from the shelter, if it is three months of age or older
At six months of age regardless of adoption date
questions.why: Dogs and cats three months of age or older are given a rabies vaccination before release from the shelter upon adoption.

questions.q: A dog has been in foster care for a month. What does the vaccination protocol require?
choices: Nothing further until adoption
Booster vaccinations, because it has been in care longer than three weeks
A new rabies vaccination every month
Deworming only
questions.why: Any dog or cat maintained at the shelter or in foster care for more than three weeks receives booster vaccinations.

questions.q: A member of the public asks the shelter to euthanize their healthy, adoptable pet. What does policy direct you to do?
choices: Schedule it for the next euthanasia day
Accept the animal and place it on the adoption floor without telling the owner
Refer the request to a local veterinarian, veterinary clinic, or hospital
Ask a supervisor to approve the euthanasia
questions.why: DAS does not euthanize healthy, adoptable animals at an owner's request. All such requests are referred to a local veterinarian or clinic.

questions.q: Before an adoptable animal may even be considered for euthanasia due to shelter capacity, what must be true?
choices: It has been impounded ten or more consecutive days and all required holding periods have expired
It has failed a behavior evaluation
It has been passed over at one adoption event
The kennel supervisor has an opening on the schedule
questions.why: Consideration applies only to animals impounded for ten or more consecutive days, and only after all required holding periods have expired, with shelter team and veterinary review.

categories.title: Animal Care & Handling
categories.tagline: Reading body language and staying safe

questions.q: What is the guiding principle for physically restraining any animal at RCDAS?
choices: Full control at all times, no matter the animal
The minimum effective restraint the situation requires
Two staff members for every animal
Mechanical restraint first, hands second
questions.why: Apply only as much physical control as the situation requires, and escalate technique only when a lesser approach has failed or is unsafe.

questions.q: A cat gives you a small scratch that barely breaks the skin. What do you do?
choices: Rinse it and keep working; only bites get reported
Report it to a supervisor immediately, regardless of severity
Mention it at the end of your shift if it still hurts
Only report it if the cat is a rabies suspect
questions.why: All bites and scratches are reported to a supervisor immediately, regardless of severity, with an incident report and medical evaluation as needed.

questions.q: A dog in a kennel shows whale eye, a tucked tail, flattened ears, and lip licking. What is it telling you?
choices: It is relaxed and ready to be leashed
It is fearful or stressed
It is playing
It is food motivated
questions.why: Whale eye, a tucked tail, flattened ears, lip licking, yawning, and a stiff still posture are signs of fear or stress. Adjust your approach.

questions.q: How do you approach a calm but uncertain dog?
choices: Head on with steady eye contact so it can see you
From the side, avoiding direct eye contact, offering the back of a loose hand to sniff
Quickly, before it has time to react
From behind, so it cannot see the leash
questions.why: Move slowly from the side rather than head on, avoid direct eye contact, and offer the back of a loose hand for sniffing before touching.

questions.q: Which of these is a catch pole NEVER used for?
choices: Guiding a dog's head in the direction of travel
Securing a dog before moving it to a kennel
Lifting, dragging, or choking a dog
Walking beside a dog that needs extra control
questions.why: A catch pole is a tool of guidance and control, not force. It is never used to lift, drag, or choke a dog.

questions.q: Which disease on this list can pass from shelter animals to people?
choices: Parvovirus
Canine distemper
Ringworm
Feline leukemia
questions.why: Ringworm is zoonotic, along with rabies, roundworm, salmonella, and toxoplasmosis. Wash hands after handling animals and wear gloves when health status is unknown.

questions.q: How often are puppies and kittens between six and twelve weeks old fed?
choices: Once a day
Twice a day
Three times a day
Free fed from a full bowl
questions.why: Puppies and kittens six to twelve weeks old are fed three times per day. From twelve weeks to twelve months it is twice a day, and adults at least once a day.

questions.q: When should shelter animals have access to water?
choices: At feeding times
At all times
Twice a day in summer
Whenever kennel staff finish cleaning
questions.why: All animals are offered water at all times, and new impounds are offered water while being processed.

questions.q: How long may processing of a new impound take before the animal is settled?
choices: No limit as long as the lobby is busy
No more than one hour
Up to four hours
Until the end of the shift
questions.why: Processing time for new impounds shall not exceed one hour, and the animal is offered water throughout.

questions.q: Two dogs start fighting in a kennel. What is your FIRST move?
choices: Enter the kennel and grab the collars
Stay outside the kennel and use a loud noise to distract and separate them
Spray them with the hose immediately
Wait for them to tire out
questions.why: Stay calm, stay outside the kennel, and start with a loud noise to distract. The hose comes next if fighting continues, and you enter only with help from other staff if necessary.

questions.q: A dog has now been in two fights. Where does it go?
choices: Back with its kennelmate once things calm down
In a kennel alone, with a handwritten Dog Aggressive note on its card
Straight to the euthanasia list
To whichever kennel has space
questions.why: A dog with two or more fights is removed, kenneled alone, and gets a handwritten Dog Aggressive note on the card, with notes entered in Chameleon.

questions.q: After any dog fight, when do notes go into Chameleon?
choices: Immediately after the fight
By the end of the week
Only if an animal was injured
Only if the public witnessed it
questions.why: Notes are entered in Chameleon immediately after a dog fight. They drive kennel decisions and keep the next handler safe.

questions.q: How is an adoption counseling conversation supposed to feel to the adopter?
choices: Like a pass or fail screening interview
Like a conversational discussion with open ended questions, focused on finding a successful match
Like a paperwork review
Like a background investigation
questions.why: Counselors use a conversational approach with open ended questions about lifestyle and expectations. The checklist guides the conversation and provides education; it is not a rigid test the applicant must pass.

questions.q: When may a visitor start viewing adoptable animals?
choices: Only after completing an Adoption Application
Before completing an Adoption Application
After a counselor is assigned to them
Only during scheduled viewing hours
questions.why: Visitors may view animals before completing an application, and any available counselor can assist them; visitors are not assigned to a specific counselor.

questions.q: A visitor wants a specific dog. What does the counselor check in Chameleon first?
choices: That the dog is available for adoption and free of holds
The adoption fee balance for the day
Whether the dog has a cute intake photo
The visitor's past donation history
questions.why: The counselor confirms the animal is available for adoption and free of holds, then reviews its information to support the matching discussion.

questions.q: Who must evaluate an animal before it leaves the shelter for a foster placement?
choices: Any volunteer who has fostered before
The Shelter Operations Chief or designee and a shelter veterinarian or designee
The front desk lead
Only the Foster Care Coordinator
questions.why: Both the Shelter Operations Chief or designee and a shelter veterinarian or designee must confirm suitability for fostering, and the review is documented in Chameleon.

questions.q: When must foster program paperwork be completed?
choices: Within a week of placement
Before the animal is removed from RCDAS premises
At the end of the foster period
Only for medical fosters
questions.why: All foster paperwork is completed before an animal leaves RCDAS premises, with questions escalated to the Foster Care Coordinator.

questions.q: An employee fosters a litter of kittens. What happens when the kittens are ready for adoption?
choices: The employee receives first priority to adopt the animals in their care, with standard fees and legal limits applying
The employee adopts them free of charge
The kittens go to the public adoption floor and the employee waits in line
Employees are not allowed to adopt their foster animals
questions.why: Employee foster caregivers get first priority to adopt animals in their care at the end of the foster period. Standard adoption fees and legal limits still apply.

categories.title: Call Center & Customer Service
categories.tagline: Bite calls and public safety on the phone

questions.q: A caller says a dog is attacking someone right now. What priority is this call?
choices: Priority 5, standard bite investigation
Priority 3, same day response
Priority 1, emergency
Refer the caller to 211
questions.why: If the animal is currently attacking or loose and aggressive, the call is treated as a Priority 1 emergency.

questions.q: A caller reports a bite that happened yesterday. The dog is contained in the owner's yard. What priority is this?
choices: Priority 1
Priority 2
Priority 5
No activity is needed if the dog is contained
questions.why: A bite that has already occurred, where the animal is contained or its location is known, proceeds as a Priority 5 bite investigation.

questions.q: The Rabies Control Desk is unavailable and you have a bite call on the line. What do you do?
choices: Ask the caller to call back later
Collect the required information and create an Activity
Transfer the call to any supervisor
Take a callback number only
questions.why: When the Rabies Control Desk is unavailable, you collect the required information and create an Activity in Chameleon so the report is not lost.

questions.q: A caller has questions about specimen results and quarantine completion. Where do those questions go?
choices: Email the Rabies Control desk at rabiescontrol@rivco.org
Give the caller the shelter veterinarian's cell number
Tell them results are posted on the website
Answer from memory based on similar cases
questions.why: Specimen results, quarantine completion confirmations, and complex rabies questions are emailed to the Rabies Control desk at rabiescontrol@rivco.org.

questions.q: How do you verify jurisdiction for a bite report?
choices: By the caller's home address in every case
By the owner of the biting animal, or by the bite location if owner information is not available
By whichever shelter is closest
Jurisdiction does not matter for bites
questions.why: Verify jurisdiction by the owner of the animal responsible for the bite. If owner information is not available, verify it by the location where the bite occurred.

questions.q: Which memo template is used when documenting a bite in the activity window?
choices: Owner Info
Bite Details
Incident Summary
Rabies Watch
questions.why: The activity includes a memo using the Bite Details template, with the required information entered in order in the comment section.
